preFix.title: Reclaiming Homemaker as Feminist Identity
preFix.subtitle: A reading on what gets lost when we treat running a home as beneath us
preFix.excerpt: A reading on the feminist case for taking homemaking seriously. Looks at the historical devaluation of domestic work, why the 'I do more than just run a home' defence backfires, and what a freely chosen domestic life can mean for women who choose it.
preFix.sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Draws on second-wave feminist critique of domestic labour (Friedan, 1963; Oakley, 1974), third-wave reassessment of choice feminism, and the contemporary reclamation of homemaking as skilled, intentional work.

content.text: A reading on what happens when we treat running a home as a consolation prize. The cultural messaging that homemaking is lesser work is so pervasive that many women who actively love their domestic lives still describe them apologetically. This reading asks where that apology comes from and whether it is worth keeping.
What the old critique got right

Second-wave feminism made a necessary argument. When the home was the only sphere available to women. The domestic role was not a choice but an assignment. A woman who spent her energy on the home and had no access to economic independence, public life. Or professional identity was not living a full life by any reasonable measure. The critique of that arrangement was correct.

The problem was how the critique travelled. What started as an argument for access and choice hardened. Over decades, into a hierarchy: paid work is serious work; domestic work is what you do instead. The woman who chose the home after the options were opened became, in some circles, suspect. Surely she had internalised her own oppression. Surely she was settling.
What it got wrong

The hierarchy of serious/lesser work carries its own assumptions. It says that a skill exercised in the home counts for less than the same skill exercised in the market. A woman who manages a household budget. Coordinates the logistics of several lives. Makes things from scratch, maintains the physical and emotional fabric of a family. And creates an environment where people can rest and recover is doing skilled, complex, and consequential work. that it is unpaid does not make it unskilled.

The economic argument for domestic work being 'free' rests on the assumption that it has no market value. This is not true. The replacement cost of domestic labour, were it paid at market rates, would be much. What is true is that it is structurally invisible, which is a different problem.

There is also a class dimension that the critique often missed. Professional women escaping the home in the 1970s frequently escaped into paid work while other women cleaned their offices and raised their children. The liberation of one group of women was often subsidised by the low-paid domestic labour of another. 'Choosing' to leave the home was, and is, a choice available to some women more than others.
The apology pattern

The apology sounds like this: 'I just run the house. But I used to work in.' or 'I'm only at home right now.' Both phrases say the same thing. Running a home is not enough on its own. It needs something added before it counts.

The defence that 'I do more than just the house' concedes the ground. If running a home well is something you have to add other things to before it counts. You have already accepted that it doesn't count on its own. The defence is not the answer to the hierarchy. It confirms the hierarchy.

Women who love their domestic lives. And who have chosen them from a position of real options. Sometimes carry more shame about that choice than women who work full-time outside the home. The shame has been handed down. It arrived in the packaging of liberation.
The freely chosen version

A freely chosen domestic life, for women who have real alternatives, looks like this. A woman who is good at running a home and finds the work satisfying. Who makes things from scratch because she likes what that produces. Who cares about the physical environment her family lives in. Who keeps a well-stocked larder as a form of preparedness. Who takes real pleasure in a well-organised house.

That woman is not failing feminism. She is using the options feminism opened. The point was that she should be able to choose. If the choice is only considered valid when it is the professional one. The choice was never really the point.

This is not an argument against paid work. Or for a return to any arrangement where domestic life is women's only option. It is an argument against the reflex that takes the word 'homemaker' and hears a smaller life. The skill and attention required to run a home well. And to care for the people in it, is not smaller than the skills required in most workplaces. It is differently placed.
One question worth sitting with

When you describe what you do at home, what words do you use? Do you include it as a full account of your work. Or do you offer it as a partial account that needs supplementing? Notice whether the apology is present, and where it came from.
Where this comes from

Written for this site. The reading draws on the feminist debate around domestic labour. Earlier sources include Betty Friedan and Ann Oakley. Later writing on choice and class includes bell hooks and Barbara Ehrenreich.
